retrieved_docs.title: Varicose Vein Treatment: Endovenous Laser Therapy -- Technique or Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: Inject local 1% lidocaine superficial to the access site under US guidance. Access the vein with a needle. Thread the guidewire through the needle and advance to the saphenofemoral junction.

retrieved_docs.title: Varicose Veins -- Treatment / Management -- Endovascular Management
retrieved_docs.content: Catheter-based endovenous ablations have been extensively used for EVTL, either via laser or radiofrequency. The procedure is preferably undertaken with local anesthetics. However, oral anxiolytics might be added for apprehensive patients. A catheter is introduced in an antegrade fashion for a few centimeters away from the saphenofemoral or saphenopopliteal junction. The catheter course within the saphenous vein is completely anesthetized. Next, the saphenous vein would be ablated by either heat energy or radiofrequency during the catheter withdrawal. [43] [44]

retrieved_docs.title: Varicose Vein Treatment: Endovenous Laser Therapy -- Technique or Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: Withdraw fiber optic laser and catheter at a rate of 1-2 mm/s while confirming ablation with the US in the longitudinal view. Take care not to heavily compress the area superficial to the catheter.

retrieved_docs.content: Return table to the neutral position. Monitor for any perioperative complications. [14]

retrieved_docs.title: Varicose Vein Treatment: Endovenous Laser Therapy -- Anatomy and Physiology
retrieved_docs.content: Commonly targetted/involved veins of the lower extremity include: Greater saphenous vein (GSV) Small saphenous vein (SSV) Saphenofemoral junction (SFJ) Inferior epigastric vein (IEV)

retrieved_docs.title: Varicose Vein Treatment: Endovenous Laser Therapy -- Technique or Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: Remove the needle and replace it with a dilator. Using a scalpel, make an incision at the junction of guidewire and skin that allows the dilator to pass smoothly.

retrieved_docs.title: Varicose Vein Treatment: Endovenous Laser Therapy -- Technique or Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: Place the patient in prone, reverse Trendelenburg position and place monitors for EKG and pulse oximetry. Identify cannulation access site on the leg, usually below the knee. Prep with antiseptic

retrieved_docs.title: Varicose Vein Treatment: Endovenous Laser Therapy -- Technique or Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: Place the patient in Trendelenburg position and inject tumescent anesthesia using an injection pump into the perivenous fascia beginning from the access site to SFJ. Typically, injections are spaced 3-5 cm apart.

retrieved_docs.title: Varicose Vein Treatment: Endovenous Laser Therapy -- Preparation
retrieved_docs.content: All patients should have a pre-operative duplex ultrasound mapping of leg veins to identify the location of reflux, vein diameter, the velocity of reflux, and patency of vein. Reflux time greater than 500 milliseconds is considered abnormal. Ultrasound mapping can be used to reveal abnormal venous anatomy, the origin of venous varicosity, and perforating veins as well as to evaluate the deep venous system. This will help serve as a roadmap.

retrieved_docs.title: Varicose Vein Treatment: Endovenous Laser Therapy -- Contraindications
retrieved_docs.content: Acute deep vein thrombosis Arterial insufficiency Acute skin infection at the site of entry Obstruction of the deep vein where the target vein serves as collateral Pregnancy One should proceed with caution in patients with post-thrombotic syndrome and venous reflux with superimposed arterial-venous fistula. Imaging to assess the patency of the deep veins is critical.

retrieved_docs.title: Varicose Vein Treatment: Endovenous Laser Therapy -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: Skin burns: Ablating superficial veins close to the skin surface can cause full-thickness burns, but the frequency of complication significantly decreases to zero with better application of tumescent anesthesia. It can be treated with local wound care and monitoring for infection.

retrieved_docs.title: Varicose Vein Treatment: Endovenous Laser Therapy -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: Thrombosis: Endovenous health-induced thrombosis occurs when a thrombus extends from the ablated greater saphenous vein into the deep femoral vein. It is differentiated into four types: type 1 at the junction of a superficial and deep vein; type 2 located in deep vein with partial occlusion (<50%); type 3 occlusion (50%-99%); type 4 complete occlusion. Incidence of endovenous health-induced thrombosis was found to be 1.4%, deep vein thrombosis was 0.3%, and pulmonary embolus was in 0.1% of cases. [15]

retrieved_docs.title: Varicose Vein Treatment: Endovenous Laser Therapy -- Technique or Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: Replace dilator with catheter and advance to SFJ. Replace the guidewire with a laser fiber. Using the US in longitudinal view, advance the laser tip up until 2 cm distal to the SFJ, ensure that the IEV is visualized.

retrieved_docs.title: Varicose Vein Treatment: Endovenous Laser Therapy -- Technique or Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: Gently sedate the patient.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 2 -- Community 1231
retrieved_docs.content: Incision of blood vessel, Incision, excision, and occlusion of vessels, and Varicose vein ligation and stripping are interrelated surgical concepts that form a hierarchical procedural framework. Incision of blood vessel represents a basic operative action that is encompassed within the broader category of Incision, excision, and occlusion of vessels, which includes a range of techniques for accessing, removing, or sealing vascular structures. Within this category, Varicose vein ligation and stripping is a specific procedure that applies the principles of incision, excision, and occlusion to treat incompetent veins. The direct procedural site of Varicose vein ligation and stripping is the vein (vény), meaning the surgery is performed on the venous tissue itself. In addition to this direct target, the procedure has an indirect effect on blood flow (إراقة الدم), as ligating and stripping the vein alters the circulation of blood through the treated vascular segment. Understanding the hierarchical relationship among these procedures and their direct and indirect anatomical targets is essential for clinicians planning vascular interventions and for optimizing patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 2158
retrieved_docs.content: Varicose vein ligation and stripping is a surgical technique that is performed directly on veins, specifically targeting the abnormal, dilated vessels known as varicose veins. The procedure involves ligating (tying off) the affected vein to prevent blood flow and then stripping (removing) the vein segment, thereby alleviating symptoms such as pain, swelling, and cosmetic concerns. By acting directly on the vein, this intervention addresses the underlying venous insufficiency that leads to varicosities and contributes to improved lower‑extremity circulation and patient quality of life.

Together, these relationships clarify both the anatomical focus of the procedure and its classification within varicose‑vein treatment strategies, supporting precise procedural planning and patient care for lower‑extremity venous disease.
